narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30 and an LSAT score of 33.50, you've worked hard to build a strong academic foundation, including middle-class family support and part-time enrollment plans, while ranking in the 5th decile for your first-year grades and 4th decile in your third year. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours, estimated a 55.9% chance that you would fail the bar—crossing the threshold that led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes candidates likely to pass on their first try. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your choice of part-time status, which is uncommon (only about 7% of students opt for it) and stood out as the most influential concern for the model in your case. On a brighter note, your LSAT score and undergraduate GPA were strong positives, each pulling the prediction toward success and performing better than many applicants. Your middle-income background and lower decile rankings in later school years added some moderate pressure toward the failure side, though less so than your enrollment choice. Compared to typical students—who average higher decile placements around 5th to 6th, LSAT scores near 37, and mostly full-time status—your profile showed competitive grades but was weighed down by the part-time path and slightly below-average test performance. This combination made the model cautious about your bar exam odds. While disappointing, this isn't a judgment on your potential; it's just one data-driven view. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, exploring full-time options if feasible, or strengthening your later-year grades to shift future predictions. You might also seek bar prep resources early or apply to programs with more flexible criteria. Keep pushing—you have the grit to redefine this story. We're rooting for your next steps.
